content: Tyrannosaurus Rex was approximately 12 to 13 metres long — about three cars parked end-to-end. It stood 4 metres tall at the hip and weighed up to 8 tonnes — heavier than an elephant. Its skull alone was 1.5 metres long, and its teeth were the size of bananas, each capable of crushing with 35,000 to 57,000 Newtons of force.

T-Rex was an apex carnivore that hunted Triceratops, Edmontosaurus, and other large dinosaurs. Its vision was excellent — better than a modern eagle — and it could smell prey from kilometers away. Despite popular jokes, its arms were quite muscular, each capable of lifting 180 kilograms, though they were disproportionately short compared to its body.

66 million years ago, a massive asteroid about 10 kilometres wide struck Earth near what is now the Yucatan Peninsula in Mexico, creating the Chicxulub crater. The impact was millions of times more powerful than any nuclear bomb. Dust and ash blocked sunlight for years, causing temperatures to plummet. About three-quarters of all species on Earth went extinct, including all non-avian dinosaurs.

Modern paleontology has revealed that many dinosaurs, including close relatives of T-Rex, had feathers. Whether adult T-Rex had full feather coverage is debated — fossil skin impressions show scales on some body parts. However, smaller relatives like Velociraptor definitely had feathers. Birds are the direct descendants of theropod dinosaurs, making them living dinosaurs.

T-Rex lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 68 to 66 million years ago. This era featured warm global temperatures, high sea levels, and diverse ecosystems. North America was split by a shallow sea, and T-Rex roamed the western landmass called Laramidia. The continent looked very different from today.

The first partial T-Rex skeleton was discovered by Barnum Brown in 1902 in Montana, USA. The most complete T-Rex skeleton, nicknamed 'Sue', was found in South Dakota in 1990 and is about 90% complete. Sue is 12.3 metres long, stands 4 metres tall at the hip, and is displayed at the Field Museum in Chicago.

T-Rex had the strongest bite force of any land animal ever measured — estimated at 35,000 to 57,000 Newtons. For comparison, a lion's bite force is about 4,500 Newtons. T-Rex could crush bone, and fossilised droppings (coprolites) contain fragments of crushed bone, confirming this bone-crushing ability.

Scientists estimate T-Rex could run at speeds of 17 to 40 kilometres per hour. While not the fastest dinosaur, its combination of size, power, and sensory abilities made it a formidable predator. Recent biomechanical studies suggest T-Rex walked with a surprising bounce in its step, similar to modern flightless birds.
